blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: How do you make it safe? How do you actually do that — without forcing it?

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: You already know part of the answer. You told her you notice. You told her you're not pushing. You told her you'll wait.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: That's not enough, though. Saying the words isn't the same as creating the condition.
Mr. Alvarez looked at him with something close to approval.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: No. It isn't. The condition is made by what you do in the ordinary moments. Not in the conversations about the difficult thing — those are too heavy to carry truth well. In the ordinary moments. When she laughs and you laugh with her and neither of you reaches for the weight of it. When you bring her coffee and don't watch her face for signs of what she might be hiding. When you sit beside her and your presence does not require anything from her. Truth comes through doors that have been opened slowly.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: And if it takes a long time?

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: Then it takes a long time. The alternative is to break the door.
He said it simply, without drama.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: You cannot receive what someone is not ready to give. You can only make yourself the kind of person they trust to give it to. The rest is not in your hands.
Ethan sat with that for a long moment. The coffee had gone cool. He didn't pick it up.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: My father left when I was seven.
He hadn't planned to say it. It arrived the way things sometimes did — not from a decision but from a quiet that had gotten large enough to hold it.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: He didn't say goodbye. He didn't give us the weight of it without the shape. He just — stopped being there. And for a long time, I thought the lesson was: don't get too close to things that can leave. Keep one foot out. Be the person who decides to go before the other person does.
Mr. Alvarez was very still.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: And then she was sitting on that wall, and I didn't keep both feet in the same place, and I don't know if that's — I don't know if it means I've finally figured something out, or if I'm just repeating something I swore I wouldn't.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: What you are doing is not the thing your father did.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: How do you know?

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: Because a man who is preparing to leave does not come and sit across from someone and ask how to stay. He does not worry about what the difficult thing is. He does not wonder whether he will be enough. He simply goes.
A long beat. The grinder. The door. The ordinary sounds of the place doing what it always did.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: You are not your father, Ethan. You are a man who stopped at a wall because a woman once sat on it. That is not a man with one foot out the door. That is a man who has already arrived.

Ethan stood. He wasn't sure when he'd decided to — his body made the decision before he had. He went around the table and put his arms around the older man, who sat very still for a moment and then placed one hand on his back, the way a father would, the way a man does when he has been trusted with something he knows the weight of.
He held on longer than he meant to. Mr. Alvarez let him.

When Ethan stepped back, he didn't say anything. Neither did Mr. Alvarez — not immediately. He looked at Ethan with a steadiness that had nothing to do with distance and everything to do with clarity.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: You are a good man.
He said it quietly. As a fact, not a comfort.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: She chose well.
